critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design. I'll work through the genuine methodological concerns systematically rather than manufacture issues.

**Issue 1: The unlabeled test set creates an unresolvable evaluation problem**

The methodology states that unlabeled alerts are excluded from training and validation but included in the test set to 'reflect operational reality.' This is the most serious flaw in the design. NDCG@10 requires relevance judgments for items in the ranked list. If unlabeled alerts appear in the test set and are treated as relevance=0 (benign), the ranking metric will be artificially inflated when the model ranks labeled-malicious alerts above unlabeled ones — even if some unlabeled alerts are genuinely malicious. Conversely, if unlabeled alerts are excluded from the NDCG computation but included in the ranking, the denominator changes implicitly: a model that ranks unlabeled items into positions 1–9 and one confirmed malicious item at position 10 would look identical to a model that surfaces the same confirmed malicious item at rank 1. The operational consequence (analyst sees the malicious alert) is completely different. The methodology does not specify how unlabeled alerts are handled in metric computation, which means the evaluation design has a silent failure mode: the metric may not measure what it claims to measure. Evidence one way or the other: show the distribution of unlabeled vs. labeled alerts within top-10 positions for both models. If unlabeled alerts frequently occupy top positions, the metric is masking a real operational problem.

**Issue 2: LTR requires a query/group structure that may not exist or is implicitly assumed**

LambdaMART and listwise rankers require alerts to be grouped into 'queries' — lists within which items are compared and ranked. The methodology mentions 'incident groups or time windows' but does not define this grouping precisely. This is not a minor implementation detail; it is the core prerequisite for the ranking objective to function. If incidents are used as groups, then the ranker only optimizes ordering within confirmed incidents — it has no mechanism to learn that a malicious alert in one incident should score higher than a benign alert from a different, concurrent time window. The operational triage problem, however, is global ranking across all alerts arriving in a shift. The group structure used during training may not correspond to the global ranking problem being evaluated. If time windows are used instead, the window size is a hyperparameter with no principled choice described, and different window sizes fundamentally change what the model learns to optimize. Evidence: compare NDCG@10 computed within-incident-group vs. globally across the full test period. If global NDCG is substantially lower than within-group NDCG, the model has learned within-incident ordering but not the operational task. Also: report what fraction of training groups contain more than one positive, since groups with a single positive provide no ranking gradient.

**Issue 3: Historical disposition rate as a feature creates a non-stationarity trap**

The 'historical disposition rates' computed on training data only are used as features. This is described as a leak-prevention measure, but it introduces a different problem: the disposition rate for a given rule ID computed over months 1–9 may be systematically different from the true rate during months 13–18 if analyst behavior, rule tuning, or attacker activity changed. More concretely, if a rule that was rarely triggered in training suddenly becomes highly active in the test period (a new attack campaign), its historical disposition rate feature is stale and potentially misleading. Both models share this feature, so it cannot explain a difference between them — but it could be the dominant signal, masking whether the ranking objective provides any benefit at all. If disposition rate alone nearly solves the problem, the comparison between ranker and classifier becomes meaningless because both are riding the same strong prior. Evidence: train both models with and without historical disposition rate (the ablation for text features is described, but this analog ablation is not). Report NDCG@10 with and without this feature for both models.

**Issue 4: The baseline comparison conflates ranking objective with calibration quality**

The baseline is a logistic regression classifier ranked by confidence score. The claim is that this comparison isolates 'the effect of the ranking formulation and joint optimization objective.' But logistic regression confidence scores are not calibrated rankings — they are posterior probability estimates. LambdaMART directly optimizes NDCG, which means it is optimized for the test metric, while logistic regression is optimized for log-loss and then its output is repurposed as a ranking score. This is not a fair comparison of 'classification vs. ranking formulation' — it is a comparison of 'metric-aligned training objective vs. misaligned training objective.' A fairer baseline would be a gradient-boosted classifier (same model family as LambdaMART) trained with a classification objective and ranked by its score, which controls for model capacity and feature interaction. Alternatively, the logistic regression could be replaced with a calibrated probability model evaluated by rank correlation rather than confidence score. As designed, any improvement attributed to the 'ranking formulation' is confounded with the model capacity difference between logistic regression and gradient boosting. Evidence: add a GBM classifier baseline with the same hyperparameter budget. If the ranker beats GBM-classifier by the same margin as it beats logistic regression, the formulation claim is supported. If the gap shrinks substantially, model capacity explains most of the difference.

**Issue 5: The test period is six months of a single enterprise — confidence intervals via bootstrap may be misleading**

Bootstrap confidence intervals over test incidents assume that incidents are exchangeable — that resampling them gives a valid estimate of uncertainty about the metric. In a single enterprise's six-month window, incidents are not exchangeable: they are temporally correlated (campaigns span days to weeks), they share infrastructure, and the overall alert distribution shifts across the period. Bootstrap over non-exchangeable units produces intervals that are too narrow, giving false precision to the comparison. The methodology does report early vs. late test period comparison for drift, which partially addresses this, but the reported 95% CIs will be systematically overconfident. This is not a reason to reject the experiment — it is a reason to not treat a barely-significant result as definitive. Evidence: compare bootstrap CIs to block-bootstrap CIs (blocks of contiguous time) to quantify how much temporal correlation inflates the standard error. If block-bootstrap CIs are substantially wider, the significance of the comparison should be revisited.

**Summary of genuine issues:**
Issues 1 and 2 are the most serious because they may cause the primary metric to not measure the operational task at all. Issue 4 is genuine but partially mitigated if GBM baseline is added. Issues 3 and 5 are real but affect both models equally (issue 3) or affect interpretation of significance (issue 5) rather than invalidating the direction of the result.

The temporal split, preprocessing discipline, ablation structure for text features, and scope claims are all correctly designed. The methodology is stronger than most applied ML papers in this domain.
all_issues_raised: Unlabeled alerts in the test set create an unresolvable NDCG computation problem: treating them as relevance=0 inflates the metric if the ranker pushes them below labeled positives, and their presence in ranked positions can mask operational failures.

The LTR group/query structure (incident groups vs. time windows) is undefined, but it fundamentally determines what the ranker optimizes — within-incident ordering is a different task from global triage ranking across all concurrent alerts.

Historical disposition rate computed on training data is a potentially dominant feature that may mask whether the ranking objective provides any independent benefit; no ablation for this feature is described.

The baseline conflates ranking formulation with model capacity: logistic regression vs. LambdaMART optimizes different objectives with different model families, so improvements cannot be attributed to the ranking formulation alone without a GBM classifier baseline.

Bootstrap confidence intervals over a single enterprise's temporally correlated incidents will be overconfident; block-bootstrap is needed to correctly estimate uncertainty in the head-to-head comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: Unlabeled alerts in the test set create an unresolvable NDCG computation problem: treating them as relevance=0 inflates the metric if the ranker pushes them below labeled positives, and their presence in ranked positions can mask operational failures.

The LTR group/query structure (incident groups vs. time windows) is undefined, but it fundamentally determines what the ranker optimizes — within-incident ordering is a different task from global triage ranking across all concurrent alerts.

Historical disposition rate computed on training data is a potentially dominant feature that may mask whether the ranking objective provides any independent benefit; no ablation for this feature is described.

The baseline conflates ranking formulation with model capacity: logistic regression vs. LambdaMART optimizes different objectives with different model families, so improvements cannot be attributed to the ranking formulation alone without a GBM classifier baseline.

Bootstrap confidence intervals over a single enterprise's temporally correlated incidents will be overconfident; block-bootstrap is needed to correctly estimate uncertainty in the head-to-head comparison.
